coredata.dc:title: Attention-based BILSTM for the degradation trend prediction of lithium battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2022 2nd International Joint Conference on Energy and Environmental Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jielong
Liu, Meijun
Luo, Peng
Chen, Xia
Yu, Hui
Wei, Xian
coredata.dc:description: There is an irreversibility in the decline of Li-ion batteries, and the performance of individual cells in the battery pack will gradually decline as the number of times the on-board Li-ion battery is charged and discharged increases This situation can significantly affect the daily use of electric vehicles, for example by shortening the driving range, and in addition, the deterioration of the battery performance increases the probability of electric vehicle breakdowns. Very little work has been done on the prediction of lithium battery performance degradation in long-mileage states, accurate prediction of future battery performance degradation can significantly reduce the probability of EV failure, making battery performance prediction very important. In this paper, we propose a BILSTM network based on an attention mechanism and utilize grey relation analysis and empirical modal decomposition in the input link of the network to address the shortcomings exposed by deep learning in the work on temporal prediction. The adopted approach can effectively address the impact of data noise and redundant features on the prediction work that occurs in deep learning. According to the experimental results, the prediction performance of the model proposed in this paper is found to be higher than other networks in both types of data sets
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery performance
Bi-directional long and short-term memory
Attention mechanism
Grey relation analysis
Empirical modal decomposition

Different model voltage difference prediction results and future forecast (Non-public data).
Comparison of the errors of different models.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2nd International Joint Conference on Energy and Environmental Engineering, CoEEE 2022, 24- 26 June, 2022, Stockholm, Sweden

head.ce:title: Attention-based BILSTM for the degradation trend prediction of lithium battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure, Fuzhou Fujian 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure, Fuzhou Fujian 350002

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Science Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information of China, Fuzhou Fujian 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Science Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen Fujian 361024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Xiamen University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen Fujian 361024, CHINA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure, Fuzhou Fujian 350002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure, Fuzhou Fujian 350002

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an irreversibility in the decline of Li-ion batteries, and the performance of individual cells in the battery pack will gradually decline as the number of times the on-board Li-ion battery is charged and discharged increases This situation can significantly affect the daily use of electric vehicles, for example by shortening the driving range, and in addition, the deterioration of the battery performance increases the probability of electric vehicle breakdowns. Very little work has been done on the prediction of lithium battery performance degradation in long-mileage states, accurate prediction of future battery performance degradation can significantly reduce the probability of EV failure, making battery performance prediction very important. In this paper, we propose a BILSTM network based on an attention mechanism and utilize grey relation analysis and empirical modal decomposition in the input link of the network to address the shortcomings exposed by deep learning in the work on temporal prediction. The adopted approach can effectively address the impact of data noise and redundant features on the prediction work that occurs in deep learning. According to the experimental results, the prediction performance of the model proposed in this paper is found to be higher than other networks in both types of data sets

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) becoming more and more popular, the development of power battery packs is getting more and more important [1]. As the most critical power source for BEV, Li-ion batteries determine the overall performance, production cost and profitability of the vehicles. In recent years, accidents caused by the failure of BEV battery systems have been reported, causing consumers to worry about the safety and reliability of BEV. As the mileage of BEV increases, the performance of the lithium battery pack will gradually deteriorate, and this deterioration is irreversible, resulting in a failure to fully charge the vehicle, a shortened driving range, and a significant increase in the probability of battery fault. The prediction of lithium battery degradation trends can ensure drivers to extend battery pack performance by adjusting driving habits, and effectively avoid battery pack failures caused by battery degradation, which is essential to protect the lives and properties of drivers [2].

Traditional methods used in data prediction are regression methods such as linear regression, Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average model (ARIMA). Shuaiqi Shen and Song Ci [3] used the ARIMA model to predict the cycle life of electric vehicles, although the predictions are as good as could be expected, the data used in this work is simple and has a clear linear trend. Methods such as ARIMA have to ensure that the data is stable and cannot accurately capture the complex variability between data with non-linear relationships, so the application of this method is limited. In recent years, deep learning methods have received a lot of attention and achieved a lot of results in prediction and recognition. Yongzhi Zhang and Rui Xiong [4] used Long Short-term Memory(LSTM) [5] to predict the remaining lifetime of lithium batteries, compared with an ordinary recurrent neural network, it can avoid gradient disappearance and explosion in the training process, and improve the stability of the network to a certain extent. Xiangbao Song and Fangfang Yang [6] added a convolution neural network into the LSTM to predict the state of charge (SOC) of Li-ion batteries, which improved the feature extraction ability of the network and further improves the model prediction accuracy. Compared with traditional methods, deep learning has a great improvement on the ability to fit the network parameters, the more complex the parameters are, the more obvious the advantages are, but the network itself cannot eliminate the redundant signal and data noise, which will affect the prediction performance and stability of the network to some extent.

Based on the problems of above-mentioned that tend to arise from deep learning in prediction work, this paper proposes an attention-based Bi-directional Long and Short-term Memory (BILSTM) prediction model and combined these two processing methods, Grey Relation Analysis (GRA) and Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD). The predictive performance of the method is validated on both open-source and the real BEV driving datasets. The contributions of this paper have three main parts:

1. GRA adopted to filter out features with low correlation to fault features and select non-redundant features as input to the network. The elimination of redundant features can reduce the computational effort of the network and reduce the occurrence of overfitting, improving the accuracy of the model, furthermore, Using EMD to decompose the fault features to obtain multiple components, this method separates out the irregular components of the fault features and reduces the noise present in the data, in conjunction with GRA, and minimize the impact of data variation on the prediction accuracy of the model.

2. The attention-based BILSTM network framework is used, and adds a layer of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to the BILSTM. Compared with other networks, this network can effectively avoid gradient disappearance and explosion during the training process, and BILSTM with convolution and pooling layers are more efficient capture the before and after features of the battery data compared to LSTM [7–9], further improving the feature extraction ability. By adding an attention mechanism to the output part of the network, the ability of the network to capture useful information can be improved by reasonably assigning weights, further improving the prediction accuracy of the model.

3. In this paper, two types of datasets are collected to validate the superiority of the proposed method, where the real BEV driving dataset has more uncertainty and significant variability characteristics, which better highlights the stability and predictive effect of the method. In addition, quantitative prediction of the voltage difference, down to the minute-by-minute voltage difference, can be achieved by adding up the distance travelled, which is somewhat advanced.

The paper is framed in four main sections. Section 1 provides a brief introduction on traditional methods and deep learning approaches applied for battery relevant parameter prediction. Section 2 in detail how to select key parameters. Section 3 proposed network architecture, attention-based BILSTM, and explain their advantages in the prediction of battery performance degradation. Experimentations by various methods are compared and results are discussed in Section 4, which verifies the superiority of the proposed methods. Finally, conclusions and future perspectives are given in Section 5.

There is no specific parameter for battery performance. Traditional method attempt to characterize battery performance in terms of the battery capacity [10] or battery internal resistance. However, battery capacity is not available in real-time, and the internal resistance is collected through realtime measurement by the sensors, the sampling accuracy depends on the sensors, and the sampling process is a time-consuming and labour-intensive process. Therefore, battery capacity and the internal resistance are not suitable to be chosen to characterize battery performance. The degradation of battery pack performance is influenced by the complex external environment and driving habits, so it is important to analyse the changes in parameters during the driving of real electric vehicles and to select the appropriate parameters to characterize battery performance.

BEV battery packs are made up of several individual cells connected in series and parallel, and the individual cells are shipped from the factory with slight differences in internal parameters, so the more charging and discharging times, the greater the difference between the voltage poles of the individual cells, the less consistent the battery pack will be. When looking at pack data over long distances, the voltage difference across the cells is an important parameter which is easily to obtained in real vehicles. Research has found that an increase in the voltage difference of Li-ion batteries exacerbates the decay of battery capacity, which is a parameter that characterizes battery life. Therefore, the variation in the differential pressure of Li-ion battery cells has a strong correlation with battery life. In addition, the inconsistency brought about by a large voltage difference greatly affects the performance of the battery pack. In summary, the voltage difference of a single cell is considered a key feature to characterize the performance of Li-ion batteries, and the prediction of voltage difference is also a prediction of the long-term performance degradation trend of Li-ion batteries.

For data sets with short sampling intervals and large variations, BILSTM [11] is used as the basic network framework in order to further improve the sequence information feature capture capability of bi-directional data. A convolutional layer and a pooling layer are added to the front end of BILSTM to improve the overall data trend capture capability. An attention mechanism is added to the back-end of BILSTM to reasonably assign output weights and improve the predictive performance of the network. The network framework is shown in Fig. 1(A).

The initial voltage difference sequence is set an input parameters into BILSTM after being processed by the convolution and pooling layers. The convolutional layer uses a convolutional kernel for effective non-linear local feature extraction of the voltage difference data [12–14], while the pooling layer is used to compress the extracted features and generate more important feature information. After processing by the convolutional and pooling layers [15], the feature information is set an input parameters into the BILSTM. An optimized improvement of the traditional LSTM with the same neurons as the LSTM and a different structure. the BILSTM can effectively improve the memory capability of the voltage difference sequence by introducing three logic control units, namely forgetting gates, input gates and output gates to maintain and update the cell states. In Fig. 1, X denotes the input at moment t, C denotes the cell state and H denotes the hidden layer state. Neurons in the BILSTM can selectively forget and remember new information about the cell state through the forgetting gate and the memory gate, preserving this useful information and passing it on to subsequent neurons. The forgetting, remembering, and output of the information is controlled by the forgetting weight, memory weight, and output weight obtained from the previous moment H and the current X through the internal weight calculation of the neuron. BILSTM facilitates the input of forward sequence information as well as the input of backward sequence information, and can fully consider past and future information, which is beneficial to further improve the model prediction accuracy compared to LSTM.

After convolution, pooling and BILSTM of the input information, the results obtained are shown below. Where

This paper, we add an attention mechanism to the BILSTM network framework. The attention mechanism is a probability weighting mechanism that mimics the attention of the human brain [16], which assigns weights reasonably based on the size of the error between the result and that point, thus replacing the random assignment of weights by neurons. Attention-based BILSTM model is shown in Fig. 1.

In this paper, the Scaled Dot-Product Attention method from the Multiplicative Attention Mechanism is used [17]. Specifically, the calculation is divided into three steps. Step1 is to calculate the weights obtained from the dot product calculation of query and each key. Step2 is to normalize the weights with the Softmax function. Step 3 is to weight the weights and the corresponding values to get attention. Attention is calculated using the following formula.
(6)
Attention
Q , K , V
= Softmax
Q
K
T
D
k
Where the matrices of queries, keys, values are denoted Q,K,V.
Q = K = V = L
and L denotes the input of the attention layer. D is the vector dimension of queries, keys, values.
In Fig. 1, the fully connected layer is layer 2, with Dense1 output dimension being 10 and Dense2 output dimension being 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Input decisions for the attention-based BILSTM network framework
ce:section.ce:para: Both types of datasets collected in this paper are actual operating battery data from electric vehicles. The data contains SOC, temperature, mileage and speed, which are parameters that reflect the driving conditions and external environment of the BEV. How to explore whether such parameters are related to fault characteristics? In this paper, GRA [18] is used to calculate the correlation between each parameter of the battery data and the voltage difference. As can be seen from Table 1, only when the correlation is greater than 0.6, the parameter and the voltage difference are synergistic, while the rest are out of tune. In this paper, the parameters with correlation degree greater than 0.6 are considered as correlation features.

The specific method of GRA is to construct each feature in the form of
s
i
1
1

i

m
⋮
⋮

⋮

n

n

⋯

i
n
m
into a complete matrix
s
i
1
1
⋮
s
i
n
1
,determine the voltage difference sequence
s
i
0
1
s
i
0
2
⋯
s
i
0
m
, then after calculating the difference between each feature sequence and the corresponding position element of the differential pressure sequence, the maximum and minimum difference between each feature sequence and the voltage difference sequence respectively. Next, both the two-level minimum differences and the two-level maximum are determined, respectively Δ (Max) and Δ (Min). Finally, calculate the correlation coefficient according to Eq. (7) and take their average to be the final result. In Eq. (7), ρ is the discrimination coefficient and is taken as 0.5.
(7)
A
ξ
j
=
Δ
M i n
+ ρ Δ
M a x
| s
i
0
r
− s
i
j
r
|
+ ρ Δ
M a x

BILSTM has excellent feature extraction capability for time series with non-linear features such as voltage difference, but the variation of non-linearity can affect the prediction accuracy of the model. In this paper, EMD is used to decompose the voltage difference series into multiple intrinsic mode functions (IMF) and a trend term RES, which have a better signal-to-noise ratio compared with Fourier variation and wavelet variation [19]. The EMD decomposition equation is shown in Eq. (8).
(8)
V
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
I M F
t
+ R E S

The flow chart of the method proposed in this paper is shown in Fig. 2. The acquired battery dataset is first filtered for relevant features using GRA, and redundant features are rounded off, and the voltage difference sequence is decomposed by EMD to obtain several components. The voltage difference component 1 instead of the voltage difference sequence in the data, and the component 1 and non-redundant features are used as inputs to the network, and the prediction results for the component 1 are obtained after training–testing. Subsequently, component 2 is substituted for component 1 and the above operation is repeated to obtain the prediction results for component 2. The above operation is repeated to finally obtain the predicted value of each voltage difference component, which is finally superimposed on the predicted value of the voltage difference.

To validate the predictive performance of the method proposed in this paper, two types of datasets were collected. One is open source data, which is the complete battery pack data provided by the Shanghai New Energy Vehicle Public Data Collection and Monitoring Research Centre. The other is real BEV driving data, which is derived from real-time battery data collected from the onboard battery management system of a brand of BEV placed on the market. Several parameters of the real BEV driving data are highly dependent on driving behaviour and changes in driving behaviour can lead to sudden changes in parameters. As a result, the actual data collected has more illogical values than the open-source data, and changes are more frequent and of greater magnitude. These non-linear variations require the network to have even better feature extraction and timing analysis capabilities.

Open source data is complete data that has been processed and therefore does not need to be cleaned. But real BEV driving datasets, the actual data will have discrete nulls in the uploaded battery data due to abnormalities in the battery management system. The imbalance of the data will lead to the prediction model not being trained properly, so data cleaning is required. As the acquisition interval is 10HZ, the difference between adjacent values is small. Therefore, for the discrete null values in the real BEV driving dataset, the mean interpolation method is adopted, taking the average of the upper and lower values of the null values for interpolation.

Normalization has the ability to improve the speed of convergence of the loss function. In the forecasting process, the input to the model usually contains data of many different magnitudes, and normalization can eliminate the effect of differences between different magnitudes on the model. This paper uses Min–Max normalization to normalize the data to [0, 1], as shown in Eq. (9), where x represents the sample data,
X
max
represents the maximum value of the sample data and
X
min
represents the minimum value of the sample data.
(9)
x
′
=
x −
x
m i n
x
m a x
−
x
m i n

The metrics used to evaluate the model was Root Mean Square Error(RMSE), Mean Absolute Error(MAE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error(MAPE %), and the evaluation metrics are provided in Eqs. (10)–(12). The smaller these three error indicators are, the more accurate the model will be.
(10)
R M S E =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
x
i
−
y
i
2
(11)
M A E =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
i
−
x
i
|
(12)
M A P E =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
i
−
x
i
|
x
i

These formulas:
y
i
denotes the predicted value of voltage difference,
x
i
denotes the actual value of differential pressure, and N denotes the number of samples.

The experimental setting for this paper is: operating system is Linux3.10, graphics card type is NVIDIA GEFORCE RTX3080Ti, deep learning framework is Tensorflow2.91, programming language is Python3.7.

To demonstrate the advantages of the method proposed in this paper, five sets of comparison experiments were set up to compare the model proposed in the paper with the BILSTM(with EMD+GRA) model, BILSTM(with EMD) model, BILSTM model, LSTM model, and CNN-BILSTM model. To ensure the fairness of the experiment, the parameters of each network were unified: batch size was 128, Dropout was 0.6,The number of training sessions is 300, the optimizer was Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) [20], the number of neurons was 16. The best model was saved during the training process using the callback function, which was utilized for the subsequent prediction work. The first 80% of the dataset was used as the training set and the second 20% as the test set.
The correlation between each parameter and the voltage difference for both data sets is shown in Table 2 by the GRA calculation method in 3.2.

It can be seen from the data in Table 2 that, for the open source data, the correlation is less than 0.6 except for speed and total current, which are out of tune, so speed and total current are used as inputs to the network. For real BEV driving data, the three parameters with a correlation greater than 0.6, namely total current, mileage, and speed, are used as inputs to the network.

The differential pressure sequence V(t) was decomposed using EMD to obtain multiple components. Some of the components are displayed visually. Fig. 3(a) shows the components of the open source data decomposed by EMD, and Fig. 4(b) shows the components of the non-open source data. The components are sorted from top to bottom according to high frequency to low frequency. According to Fig. 3, it can be found that from IMF0 to IMF9, the trend is gradually smoothed and the change in IMF is basically the same.

Fig. 4(a), the oscillations are so sharp that it is not possible to visualize the difference between the models. In Fig. 4(b), the voltage difference is on the decline, CNN-BILSTM, BILSTM (Add EMD) and LSTM are on the down side compared to the real value. In Fig. 4(c), CNN-BILSTM is on the up side compared to the real value, LSTM, BILSTM and BILSTM (Add EMD) are the three models skewed down compared to the real values. Both the method proposed in this paper and BILSTM (Add EMD+GRA) accurately track the trend of the voltage difference and it is not possible to visualize the difference between them, which should be assessed by evaluation metrics.

In order to be able to demonstrate further the specific approach of the model proposed in this paper for prediction work, simulations were carried out using non-public data. As can be seen from the previous section, the associated features of the non-public data are total voltage, mileage and speed. For long mileage road driving, combined with the empirical belief that vehicle conditions are repetitive during use, the total voltage and speed are therefore the same as those in the training set, and the total mileage is accumulated on the basis of the previous ones. The model calculates the voltage difference of the on-board lithium battery for 254 to 303 KM. The visualization of the prediction effect of different models and the future voltage difference prediction results are shown in Fig. 5.

In Fig. 5(a), the difference between the models cannot be visualized. According to Fig. 5(b) it can be found that the predictions of BILSTM and BILSTM (Add EMD) are skewed upwards compared to the true values, and in Fig. 5(c) the LSTM does not track accurately against sudden changes. From the combined analysis of the two types of datasets, it can be found that the prediction results are improved to different degrees with the GRA+EMD treatment. Traditional methods are affected by redundant features or data noise and tend to have poorer prediction results or fail to accurately capture abrupt changes in the data. Finally, by extending the driving range and obtaining the voltage difference at 254 to 303 KM in the future, the voltage difference in case of failure can be obtained by analysing the battery lifetime data in advance and setting it as a threshold value. Using the method described in this paper, the future trend of the vehicle’s lithium battery performance can be obtained in advance and the vehicle’s on-board battery pack life can be extended by changing driving habits.

The prediction error of each model was calculated by the model evaluation function, as demonstrated in Table 3, it is found that the errors of CNN-BILSTM and LSTM are larger, which is consistent with that reflected in Fig. 4. The errors of BILSTM are reduced compared with LSTM, which proves that the bi-directional extraction of features is better than the unidirectional extraction of features. The accuracy is substantially improved after adding EMD to BILSTM, which indicates that EMD can effectively improve the prediction accuracy by decomposing the fault features and multi-component prediction. The prediction accuracy is also slightly improved by removing redundant features through GRA. Finally, the error obtained by adding the attention mechanism to the output of the network is reduced compared to BILSTM (With EMD+GRA), with RMSE, MAE and MAPE% of 1.1514/0.9237, 0.9902/0.8372 and 7.6828%/10.4520%. In summary, the method proposed in this paper has higher prediction accuracy compared to other models on both open-source and non-open-source datasets.

In this paper, an Attention-based BILSTM network framework is proposed, and convolutional and pooling layers are added before the BILSTM network to improve the feature extraction ability. Further, the GRA+EMD method is used to selectively input the parameters contained in the battery data. The best prediction results are obtained in both public and non-public datasets, and experiments show that the prediction performance of the model is improved to varying degrees using GRA, EMD and attention mechanisms. Finally, the steps of the method used in a practical prediction exercise are described, where future trends in voltage differences can be predicted by extending the driving range and repeating the periodic parameters. The search for complete battery pack data containing fault data, combined with the present method is expected to explore a new path for lithium battery life prediction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by ‘Fujian Science & Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information of China’ under Grant No. 2021ZZ120; ‘FuJian Science and Technology Plan, China’ under Grant No. 2021T3003; ‘QuanZhou Science and Technology Plan, China ’ under Grant No. 2021C065L.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new SOH prediction concept for the power lithium-ion battery used on HEVs
2009 IEEE vehicle power and propulsion conference

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Data-driven prediction of battery cycle life before capacity degradation
Nat Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Severson K A, Attia P M, Jin N, et al. Data-driven prediction of battery cycle life before capacity degradation[J]. Nature Energy, 2019, 4(5): 383-391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lifecycle prediction of second use electric vehicle batteries based on arima model
2019 ieee globecom workshops

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A LSTM-RNN method for the lithuim-ion battery remaining useful life predic- tion
2017 prognostics and system health management conference

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang Y Z, Xiong R, He H W, et al. A LSTM-RNN method for the lithuim-ion battery remaining useful life predic- tion[C]//2017 Prognostics and System Health Management Conference (PHM-Harbin). IEEE, 2017: 1-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long short-term memory
